behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit beats Integration because this failure is in statement-splitting logic at a single parser boundary; no cross-module state handoff is required by the behavior text. Unit beats System because the behavior only inspects shell text segmentation, with no OS/network/subprocess dependency. Rejection gate: Integration/System are disqualified by the behavior text focusing only on segmentation output.
behaviors.test_description: Call the segmentation function with a multiline shell snippet containing newline-separated assignments and commands; assert it returns multiple ordered segments (assignments and command split) rather than one merged segment.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit because the incident mode depends on segmentation output being handed to trigger classification (module boundary interaction), not just isolated predicate logic. Integration beats System because behavior text does not require executing `gh` or real HTTP; command-text parsing/classification is sufficient. Rejection gate: System is disqualified by 'multi-line shell block ... is recognized' (recognition, not command execution).
behaviors.test_description: Wire real segmenter + trigger classifier together. Feed a multiline block with assignments then `gh pr create ...`; assert classifier returns PR-create trigger (not `not_a_trigger`) for the relevant segment.

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit for the same boundary reason as behavior 2: correctness depends on segmentation+classification interaction under multiline input. Integration beats System because no real subprocess/network behavior is needed; this is trigger discrimination on parsed segments. Rejection gate: System disqualified by behavior wording about classification outcome only.
behaviors.test_description: Run integrated segmenter+classifier on multiline assignments followed by `gh issue create ...`; assert result is not PR-create trigger and does not open PR review loop state.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit because this behavior spans trigger handling, identifier reconstruction, and persistence (multiple modules + storage boundary). Integration beats System because it only requires local persistence semantics; no real `gh` subprocess/API call is required by the behavior text. Rejection gate: System disqualified by 'reconstruct and persist ... state' with no requirement to resolve identifier from live external infra.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp workspace, invoke post-tool hook path with a detected PR-create event fixture containing identifier data. Assert the persisted review-loop state file contains the reconstructed PR identifier in expected field format.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit because this is an inter-module contract test across post-tool writer and pre-tool reader/enforcer. Integration beats System because local file contract/read enforcement can be validated in-process with temp filesystem; no OS-special primitives, network, or subprocess required. Rejection gate: System disqualified by behavior text limiting scope to state format compatibility between hooks.
behaviors.test_description: Execute post-tool hook logic to write gate state after PR-create trigger, then execute pre-tool enforcement hook against that state; assert enforcement activates correctly (e.g., blocks or gates as designed).
